title: When the Wind Forgot to Blow
synopsis: Hugo lives in a lighthouse where the wind never stops howling—until one day it does. When mysterious gifts start arriving each day, he discovers the hilarious truth about what happens when weather breaks down completely.

pages.text: Every flag on our lighthouse pole hangs straight down like wet socks. This is wrong, wrong, WRONG! The wind at the edge of the world never stops howling, but today it sounds like someone turned off a giant fan. I press my nose to the window and watch Dad's weather vanes spin backwards, then forwards, then give up entirely. A seagull tries to fly and just drops straight into the ocean with a splash. Dad scrambles up the lighthouse stairs, his weather instruments clanking like pots and pans. "Hugo!" he shouts. "The wind has completely stopped! This is meteorologically impossible!" I nod seriously because I love big science words, even when everything is going hilariously haywire.

illustration.description: A gouache painting showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes pressing his face against a lighthouse window. Outside, flags hang limply, a seagull is falling straight down toward the ocean, and weather vanes point in confused directions. His father climbs the stairs with weather instruments scattered around him. Painted in thick brushstrokes with warm savanna colors, golden grass visible outside, and an orange sunset sky in bold confident strokes.
illustration.alt: A blonde boy at a lighthouse window watching still weather outside while his father climbs stairs with instruments

pages.text: Then I spot something impossible floating toward our lighthouse—not flying, just drifting like it's underwater. A small brown package bobs through the air, moving slower than a sleepy turtle. It bumps into our lighthouse wall three times before finding the window. When I open it, the package plops onto my hands like a dead fish. Inside is a tiny paper airplane and a note that says "Monday's Gift - From Your Weather Friends." The paper airplane immediately crashes into my soup bowl. Dad scratches his head so hard his hair stands up. "Weather friends?" he mutters. "What in the name of atmospheric pressure are weather friends?"

illustration.description: A gouache painting showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes holding a brown package at an open window. A tiny paper airplane is crashed nose-first into a bowl of soup on the table. His father stands nearby scratching his head with wild hair sticking up. The scene is painted in thick brushstrokes with warm colors, textured painterly style showing the lighthouse interior with golden light.
illustration.alt: A blonde boy holding a package while a paper airplane crashes into soup, his father looking confused

pages.text: Tuesday brings a miniature tornado in a jar that spins the wrong direction. When I unscrew the lid, it sucks up all of Dad's weather reports and shoots them out the chimney like confetti. Wednesday delivers a snow globe that snows upward—the fake snow sticks to the ceiling and spells out "OOPS" in drippy letters. Thursday's gift is a compass that points to "Maybe" and "Who Knows?" instead of north and south. Dad keeps taking notes in his weather journal, but every time he writes something down, the mini tornado sucks up his pencil. "This is scientifically impossible!" he declares, then trips over his own weather balloon that's floating backwards around the lighthouse floor.

illustration.description: A gouache painting showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes surrounded by chaotic weather gifts. A tiny tornado in a jar shoots papers everywhere, snow falls upward to the ceiling spelling 'OOPS', and a confused compass points to silly directions. His father tries to write notes while tripping over a backwards-floating weather balloon. The scene bursts with thick brushstrokes, warm savanna colors, and bold confident strokes showing the lighthouse interior in delightful chaos.
illustration.alt: A blonde boy surrounded by magical weather chaos while his father stumbles around taking notes

pages.text: Friday's package contains a thermometer that measures "Giggly" and "Grumpy" instead of hot and cold. Saturday brings a wind chime that plays music even without wind—it sounds like elephants learning to whistle. By Sunday, I've figured out the pattern. Each gift is broken weather, like someone took apart a storm and mailed us the pieces. The final package holds a tiny cloud that rains chocolate milk, but only on Sundays, and only if you ask it nicely. Dad tastes the chocolate milk and his eyes go wide. "Hugo," he whispers, "I think someone is trying to tell us something about what happened to our wind." Just then, the chocolate cloud starts giggling.

illustration.description: A gouache painting showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes holding a small cloud that drips chocolate milk into a cup. Around him are scattered the week's gifts: a silly thermometer, musical wind chimes, and other broken weather instruments. His father tastes chocolate milk with wide, surprised eyes. The scene is painted in thick brushstrokes with warm golden colors and orange sunset light streaming through lighthouse windows, using bold confident strokes and textured painterly technique.
illustration.alt: A blonde boy holding a chocolate-milk-raining cloud while his father tastes the milk, surrounded by magical weather instruments

pages.text: Monday morning, instead of a package, seven tiny cloud creatures float through our window. They look like cotton balls with googly eyes and seem very embarrassed. The biggest one—who appears to be their leader—squeaks in a voice like a helium balloon: "We're sorry! We broke your wind!" They explain that they're weather apprentices who were practicing making breezes when their wind-making machine exploded. All the wind from our part of the world got scattered into tiny pieces and mailed to us as gifts. Dad drops his coffee mug, which bounces instead of breaking because even gravity is acting silly today. "You mean," I say slowly, "the packages weren't presents—they were apology notes?" The cloud creatures nod so hard they start raining tiny apology letters.

illustration.description: A gouache painting showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes facing seven small cloud creatures with googly eyes floating in the lighthouse. The creatures look embarrassed and are raining tiny paper letters. His father has dropped a coffee mug that bounces on the floor instead of breaking. The scene is painted in thick brushstrokes with warm savanna colors, golden grass visible through windows, and orange sunset lighting in bold confident strokes with textured painterly technique.
illustration.alt: A blonde boy meeting seven googly-eyed cloud creatures who rain apology letters while his father drops a bouncing coffee mug

pages.text: The cloud creatures work together to fix their wind-making machine, which looks like a bicycle made of rainbows attached to a kazoo. They pedal furiously while humming off-key, and slowly—very slowly—a gentle breeze starts tickling my hair. Dad's weather vanes begin spinning the right direction. The backwards-floating weather balloon finally deflates. Even the seagulls manage to fly properly, though they still look confused. "Thank you for keeping our wind safe," squeaks the leader cloud. "Will you be our human weather friends?" I look at Dad, who's trying to measure "Giggly" on the broken thermometer and laughing at the result. "Yes," I say, "but next time your machine breaks, maybe just write a regular letter?" All seven clouds giggle, and somewhere far away, I hear the beautiful sound of wind returning to blow where wind should blow.

illustration.description: A gouache painting showing a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes watching seven cloud creatures operate a rainbow bicycle-kazoo machine. Wind is starting to blow again—his hair moves in the breeze, weather vanes spin correctly, and seagulls fly outside the lighthouse windows. His father measures the silly thermometer while laughing. The scene is painted in thick brushstrokes with warm savanna colors, golden grass swaying in the returning wind, and orange sunset sky in bold confident strokes with textured painterly technique.
illustration.alt: A blonde boy and his father with cloud creatures fixing their rainbow wind machine as normal weather returns to the lighthouse

coverPrompt.description: A gouache painting showing a tall white lighthouse perched on a cliff at the edge of the world, with a boy with straight light blonde hair, fair skin, and light blue eyes standing at the top holding a small package. The wind appears completely still—seabirds frozen mid-flight, grass standing perfectly straight. Golden grass surrounds the lighthouse base with warm savanna colors and thick, bold brushstrokes. An orange sunset paints the sky in textured painterly style.
coverPrompt.alt: A lighthouse on a cliff with a blonde boy holding a package, everything unnaturally still

questions.question: What was wrong with the weather at the lighthouse?
questions.explanation: The wind at the lighthouse stopped blowing, which was impossible because the wind there never stops howling.

questions.question: What did the first gift contain?
questions.explanation: Monday's gift was a tiny paper airplane that immediately crashed into Hugo's soup bowl.

questions.question: Who sent the seven packages to Hugo?
questions.explanation: Seven tiny cloud creatures who were weather apprentices sent the packages as apology gifts after they broke their wind-making machine.

questions.question: What did the cloud creatures use to fix the wind?
choices: A bicycle made of rainbows attached to a kazoo
questions.explanation: The cloud creatures used a machine that looked like a bicycle made of rainbows attached to a kazoo to make wind.

questions.question: How did Hugo feel about becoming weather friends with the cloud creatures?
choices: He said yes but asked them to write regular letters next time
He said no because they caused trouble
questions.explanation: Hugo agreed to be their weather friend but suggested they write regular letters if their machine breaks again instead of sending broken weather pieces.
